tldr: Headlines from 12 outlet buckets frame Ukraine's Fedorov dismissal as a three-register crisis: a political cabinet upheaval (reshuffle, resignations, new appointments), a mass public backlash (protests spreading to 16 cities, persisting five consecutive days), and a nascent military command crisis (Zelensky reviewing 11 candidates to replace commander Syrsky). The political and protest registers are near-universal, appearing across 11 of 12 buckets, while the security-readiness frame is confined to Estonia/Baltics, Kyiv Post, and Meduza. The Poland/Baltic outlet ERR News is the only source to frame the crisis through the lens of frontline soldiers who 'can't afford to be shaken by political upheaval' — a war-readiness concern entirely absent from Western European and American headline choices. RFE/RL uniquely surfaces civilian emotional alarm with a direct quote headline — ''What A Nightmare'' — producing the sharpest rhetorical register among non-Ukrainian sources.

evidence.quote: Ukraine : un remaniement à haut risque ?
Ukraine appoints Yevhenii Khmara as acting
Ukraine PM Svyrydenko quits in latest Zelenskyy
Fedorov out as Ukraine's defense minister
Ukraine lawmakers approve Koretskyi as new PM
Syrsky Breaks Silence, Apologizes to Ousted
Why protests are growing in Ukraine
Ukraine's army chief expected to lose job
Ukraine's soldiers can't afford to be shaken
Zelensky Weighs Replacing Syrsky With Drapaty

single_outlet_findings.finding: The only headline to reframe the political crisis as a battlefield vulnerability — 'soldiers can't afford to be shaken by political upheaval' — a war-readiness concern absent from every other outlet's title.

The only non-Ukrainian outlet to foreground civilian emotional alarm in the headline itself via direct quote ('What A Nightmare'), producing the sharpest rhetorical register in the corpus for a US Congress-funded broadcaster.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, the Fedorov dismissal is framed first as a political earthquake (cabinet reshuffle, PM resignation, a string of new appointments), second as an uncontained street backlash (protests spreading across 16 cities over five consecutive days), and third — only in Baltic and Ukrainian outlet headlines — as a genuine military command crisis threatening frontline stability.
